_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: Twelve hundred years of composure, and she has never once raised her voice.

museum_objects.title: Coenwulf Gold Mancus
museum_objects.description: An extraordinarily rare Anglo-Saxon gold coin from the successor system to Offa's silver penny standard.
museum_objects.significance: Only a handful of these survive — a reminder that behind every steady currency is a moment so fragile that almost all the evidence of it was lost to time.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: An extremely rare gold coin from Anglo-Saxon England — so rare the British Museum had to fight to keep it from being sold abroad.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

display_alternatives.title: Coenwulf, King of Mercia, gold mancus; struck 796–821 AD (obverse).png

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Cynethryth Penny (Offa's Queen)
museum_objects.description: A silver penny struck in the name of Cynethryth, wife of King Offa — the only Anglo-Saxon queen ever to have coinage struck in her own name.
museum_objects.significance: A woman's name on money, thirteen centuries before anyone thought that was normal. Charlotte's whole story starts with someone insisting she deserved to be named.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: A coin stamped with a queen's name — over 1,200 years ago, this was the only time an English queen got her own money.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

display_alternatives.title: Early-medieval coin , Penny of Cynethryth, wife of Offa, King of Mercia (FindID 197414).jpg

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Silver Penny of Offa (East Anglian mint)
museum_objects.description: A silver penny of King Offa of Mercia, struck by the moneyer Lul, establishing the 240-pennies-to-a-pound system.
museum_objects.significance: This is the coin that invented the pound as a unit — the ancestor of every British pound that would ever be spent.
museum_objects.plain_language: The actual coin design that created the math behind the British pound: 240 pennies to one pound of silver.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

display_alternatives.title: Early medieval silver penny of Offa of Mercia (Heavy coinage) c. 792-3 - 796AD. (FindID 993480).jpg

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Sutton Hoo Helmet
museum_objects.description: The iconic ceremonial helmet from the Sutton Hoo ship burial, the richest Anglo-Saxon grave ever found.
museum_objects.significance: Charlotte's world was already this rich and ceremonial before a single coin bore her likeness — this helmet is what wealth looked like before money had a face.
museum_objects.license: British Museum Standard Terms of Use
museum_objects.plain_language: A famous ancient warrior's helmet buried with a king, showing how wealthy and elaborate Anglo-Saxon England already was.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum Standard Terms of Use

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Staffordshire Hoard
museum_objects.description: The largest hoard of Anglo-Saxon gold and silver metalwork ever found, unearthed in a Staffordshire field in 2009.
museum_objects.significance: Buried in a hurry and never reclaimed — a whole kingdom's wealth left in the dirt, waiting 1,300 years to tell its story.
museum_objects.license: Contact Birmingham Museums Trust rights office
museum_objects.plain_language: The biggest pile of Anglo-Saxon gold ever discovered, found by an ordinary person using a metal detector in a farm field.
museum_objects.licence: Contact Birmingham Museums Trust rights office

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: Sylloge of Coins of the British Isles Vol. 67: British Museum Anglo-Saxon Coins II
primary_sources.author: Rory Naismith
primary_sources.description: Definitive scholarly catalogue of 1,436 Anglo-Saxon coins from Offa to Alfred.
primary_sources.plain_language: The most complete official list ever made of early English coins, cataloguing over 1,400 of them.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: Corpus of Early Medieval Coin Finds (EMC)
primary_sources.author: Fitzwilliam Museum, Cambridge
primary_sources.description: Database recording single coin finds in Britain from 410-1180 AD, with roughly 12,000 entries.
primary_sources.plain_language: A searchable database of every early English coin ever found by archaeologists or metal detectorists.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: Portable Antiquities Scheme Database
primary_sources.author: British Museum / PAS
primary_sources.description: National database recording archaeological objects found by the public, including thousands of Anglo-Saxon coins.
primary_sources.plain_language: A public database where regular people report ancient coins and objects they've dug up.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

primary_sources.title: History of the English Penny (c.600-1066)
primary_sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
primary_sources.description: General reference timeline confirming Offa's penny system remained England's monetary basis until the 14th century.
primary_sources.plain_language: An overview explaining how one king's coin design stayed in use for over 500 years.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

primary_sources.title: A Short History of the Pound
primary_sources.author: BBC News
primary_sources.description: Explainer article tracing the pound sterling's history from Anglo-Saxon origins to the present.
primary_sources.plain_language: A news article explaining, in simple terms, how the British pound got its name and history.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

world_map.map_description: Charlotte's pound sterling was born in Anglo-Saxon Mercia and grew into the currency of a global empire, then a modern financial capital.

nodes.label: Mercia (Offa's Kingdom)
nodes.description: The Anglo-Saxon kingdom where King Offa established the silver penny system that became the ancestor of the pound.

nodes.label: Canterbury Mint
nodes.description: One of Offa's most active mints, striking pennies that circulated across southern England.

nodes.label: Sutton Hoo, Suffolk
nodes.description: Site of the richest Anglo-Saxon ship burial ever found, showing the wealth of the world Charlotte was born into.

nodes.label: Offa's Dyke
nodes.description: The massive earthwork boundary built by King Offa, symbol of Mercian power and organization.

nodes.label: City of London
nodes.description: Modern financial heart of Britain and home to the Bank of England, where the pound is managed today.

nodes.label: Royal Mint, Llantrisant
nodes.description: The current home of British coin production, descended from over a thousand years of minting tradition.
